findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 41. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: Ensure the main landmark is at top level
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  The main landmark is contained in another landmark. [<main>]

findings.description: Ensure the document has at most one main landmark
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  Document has more than one main landmark [<main>]

Fix any of the following:
  The landmark must have a unique aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title to make landmarks distinguishable [<main>]

findings.description: The HTML adds internal navigation anchor links (#history-background, etc.) in the table of contents that do not exist in the source PDF. These are functional additions for HTML navigation, not fabricated external URLs.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <a href="#history-background">, <a href="#march-10-2016-public-meeting-and-oral-comments-summary">, etc. The PDF has no hyperlinks in its table of contents.

findings.description: The document has nested <main> elements - an outer <main> from the template and an inner <main> wrapping the actual content. This is an HTML structural issue but does not affect content fidelity.

The header block content (State of North Carolina | Environmental Quality | Water Resources, address, phone number, and the names/titles of Pat McCrory, Donald R. Van Der Vaart, S. Jay Zimmerman) appears in both the <header> section and also exists implicitly as part of the page footer in the PDF. The HTML consolidates it to the header only, which is acceptable. No actual content duplication issue.
findings.evidence: Footer text '1611 Mail service Center | Raleigh, North Carolina 27699-1611 | 919 707 9000' appears once in HTML header, matching the PDF letterhead.
